source.title: Anticholinergic Drug Exposure and the Risk of Dementia: A Nested Case-Control Study

confidence_rationale: Very large nested case-control (58,769 dementia cases, 225,574 controls); class-specific analysis provides mechanistic granularity; consistent dose-response with 1-11 year exposure window; population-attributable fraction of 10.3% indicates clinical significance. The class-specific breakdown (bladder antimuscarinics OR 1.65, antidepressants 1.29, antipsychotics 1.70) is particularly useful for MECH-175 as different drug classes have different REM suppression vs diurnal cholinergic profiles -- though the paper cannot disentangle these mechanistic contributions.
failure_signatures: The nested case-control design cannot establish temporal directionality beyond the 1-11 year exposure window -- anticholinergic prescribing patterns may still partially reflect prodromal dementia symptoms.
Exposure windows of 3-13 years and 5-20 years show similar ORs (1.46 and 1.44), suggesting long-duration exposure patterns but not illuminating which years of exposure are most critical.

Class-specific ORs span a range (antidepressants 1.29 to antipsychotics 1.70) that MECH-175 would predict partially from differential REM suppression power, but confounding by indication makes direct mechanistic inference difficult.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Exposure to anticholinergic drugs (56 drug types with strong anticholinergic properties) is associated with increased dementia risk in a dose-response manner; OR 1.49 (95% CI 1.44-1.54) at >1095 TSDDs; associations strongest for bladder antimuscarinics (OR 1.65), antipsychotics (OR 1.70), and antidepressants (OR 1.29). Population-attributable fraction 10.3%.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-175 predicts class-specific differential risk based on differential REM suppression power and anticholinergic burden: bladder antimuscarinics (high peripheral ACh, moderate central) and antidepressants (high central ACh, high REM suppression via tricyclics and paroxetine) would be expected to have higher ORs. The pattern is broadly consistent with MECH-175 dual-pathway predictions, though confounding by indication is substantial for antipsychotics and antidepressants.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Class-level ORs aggregate drugs with very different REM suppression profiles (e.g., amitriptyline vs SSRIs within antidepressants; oxybutynin vs mirabegron precursor drugs within bladder agents). MECH-175 predicts within-class variation based on REM suppression potency, which this study cannot test.
mapping.source_context: Nested case-control, primary care England QResearch database; adults 55+; n=284,343 total (cases + controls); computerised prescribing records; dementia diagnosis from coded records.

confidence_components.notes: Very high source quality. Mapping fidelity limited by mechanism-agnostic TSDD aggregation across heterogeneous drug classes.
